title: Analysis & Discovery
summary: Understand the audience, the gap, and the constraints before designing anything.
primer: Before you design anything, you need to understand what's actually broken. Talk to stakeholders, look at the performance data, write a real brief. Skip this part and you're just guessing — and you find out the hard way, weeks later, when nobody learns anything.

title: Strategy
summary: Decide what kind of learning experience this needs to be, and why.
primer: Strategy is the bets you make before you start designing. Modality, depth, who you're really designing for, what success looks like. I've watched people skip this and jump straight to 'let's build a course' — it almost never ends well. The decisions you make here shape the next 80% of the work.

title: Curriculum
summary: Shape the arc — modules, outcomes, the learner journey.
primer: This is where you shape the arc. What comes first, what builds on what, what gets assessed. People sometimes confuse it with design, but it's higher altitude — outcomes and sequence, not slides and activities. Get this right and the rest gets easier.

title: Design
primer: Design is where 'we'll teach X' turns into 'here's exactly how the learner experiences X.' Activities, narrative, decisions, assessment. This is the most creative stage of the work — and the most likely to spiral if the earlier brief was vague.

title: Development
summary: Build the assets — slides, video, code, copy.
primer: This is production. Slides, video, scripts, code, copy. It used to be the most time-consuming part of any project by a wide margin. With AI it doesn't have to be — most of the time-saving cards in this playbook live in this stage.

title: Plan
primer: Plan is the part nobody romanticizes. Rollout, comms, registration, scheduling, who's facilitating which session. It's the work that decides whether anyone actually shows up — and whether the first cohort feels intentional or improvised.

title: Execution
summary: Deliver the experience to real learners.
primer: Execution is where training meets reality. Live, async, hybrid — doesn't matter. The learners' actual context shows up here, and you'll see exactly which parts of your design hold up. Great execution can rescue mediocre design; bad execution can ruin great design.

title: Measurement
summary: Find out whether it worked, on the dimensions that matter.
primer: Did it actually work? Beyond completion rates and smile sheets — did behavior change? Did the business outcome move? Most projects either skip this or measure the wrong thing. The ones that get it right have something concrete to point at when they ask for the next budget.

title: Improvement
summary: Iterate. Training is a product, not a project.
primer: Treat training like a product, not a project. Read the signals from execution and measurement, decide what to iterate, ship the next version. The first cohort is rarely the best version — the second one usually is.

title: Project Management
summary: Keep the work moving — scope, schedule, stakeholders, and risk.
primer: This one isn't a phase — it runs across everything else. Scope, schedule, stakeholders, risks, decisions. The gap between 'a good design' and 'a design that actually shipped on time' is almost always project management.
